---
title: "PlanForge DAGs and Intelligence Arbitrage"
chapter_id: "planforge-dags-and-intelligence-arbitrage"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-25"
primary_sources:
  - "planforge"
  - "planforge_compiler_arch"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "cognitive_compilation"
  - "tokenmana"
  - "moecot"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Four local raw-cache mappings are passage-reviewed; `coherence_exchange` and `moecot` remain connector/source-note mapped, and support remains argument until scheduler, tier-adequacy, cost-quality, or route-trace evidence exists."
  - "Only protocol schema/fixture validation is implemented; no behavioral execution tests have been run for this chapter."
  - "Lean coverage is limited to finite indexed plan-graph and node-outcome predicates; deployed scheduler, tier-adequacy, and cost-quality proofs remain unimplemented."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# PlanForge DAGs and Intelligence Arbitrage

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `planforge-dags-and-intelligence-arbitrage` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-25 |
| Primary source records | `planforge`, `planforge_compiler_arch`, `coherence_exchange`, `cognitive_compilation`, `tokenmana`, `moecot` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `planforge`, `planforge_compiler_arch`; supporting: `coherence_exchange`, `cognitive_compilation`, `tokenmana`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `planforge`, `planforge_compiler_arch`, `coherence_exchange`, `cognitive_compilation`, `tokenmana`, `moecot`; passage-reviewed local raw cache: `planforge`, `planforge_compiler_arch`, `cognitive_compilation`, `tokenmana`; connector/source-note only: `coherence_exchange`, `moecot` |
| Test state | Protocol fixture validation and `AsiStackProofs.PlanForge` finite-record proofs are implemented; scheduler and behavioral execution tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats PlanForge as a scheduler architecture and research target. The repo validates a draft PlanForge DAG fixture and implements narrow finite-record Lean predicates for indexed dependency order and failed-quality fallback. It does not measure cost savings, measure quality retention, implement a tier-routing scheduler, or prove the behavior of a deployed graph algorithm.

## Problem

Large tasks require decomposition, scheduling, tiered model selection, and cost-aware routing.

The previous chapter defined the plan graph as the control artifact. This chapter specializes that graph into PlanForge-style DAG planning: decomposition, dependency inference, scheduling, model/tool tier assignment, cost accounting, quality predicates, escalation, and residual handling. PlanForge and the compiler-architecture variant both point to the same design pressure: orchestration should be compiled, not chatted through serially.

This chapter is also the book's first major efficiency mechanism after the Efficient ASI thesis. Intelligence arbitrage is not "use cheaper models whenever possible." It is "assign each node the minimum adequate capability plus the verification burden needed to know that adequacy was enough."

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Uniformly applying the strongest model to every task wastes compute and can obscure which tasks require verification or escalation.

Uniform routing hides structure. Some nodes are clerical, some require domain expertise, some require external tools, some require human approval, and some require adversarial review. Sending everything to the largest model wastes resources and can make the system less inspectable because no one knows which step was actually hard.

The opposite failure is under-routing: a weak worker handles a node whose quality predicate demands stronger reasoning, broader context, or independent verification. TokenMana adds the resource-governance frame: budgets are not just token counts; they include latency, review capacity, burst limits, and human cognitive cost.

## Core Claim

[planforge-dags-and-intelligence-arbitrage.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] PlanForge-style DAG planning can support intelligence arbitrage by assigning each node the minimum adequate capability and verification burden.

The claim remains at `argument` support. PlanForge and its compiler-architecture variant supply decomposition, primitive typing, MVI routing, and scheduling vocabulary. Cognitive Compilation supplies the IR and repair-locality connection. TokenMana motivates budgets as load, latency, review, and cognitive-friction constraints. The Coherence Exchange remains speculative connector context, not benchmark evidence.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C maps this PlanForge DAG claim to all assigned source notes. Four local raw-cache mappings are now passage-reviewed. The mappings support DAG decomposition, tier annotation, budget pressure, and verification burden as an architecture, not measured cost savings, tier adequacy, deployed scheduler behavior, or implemented epistemic economics. `coherence_exchange` and `moecot` remain connector/source-note mapped until usable raw text, code, logs, release artifacts, benchmark records, or external corroboration are imported or inspected.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `planforge` | Passage-reviewed: recursive decomposition, primitive action schemas, optimization, pre/postconditions, dependency inference, scheduling, MVI tier annotation, execution handoff, fallback, source-reported synthetic scenarios, watchdog checks, and failure-driven replanning. | No scheduler implementation, route trace, reproduced benchmark, or cost-quality result is present. |
| `planforge_compiler_arch` | Passage-reviewed: planner-as-compiler framing with task graphs or IR, typed primitives, dependencies, interfaces, quality gates, model/tool tier routing, cost/latency profiles, independent-node parallelism, critical-path scheduling, and source-reported estimates. | Market and cost comparisons are motivating estimates only; cost, time, and quality numbers are not reproduced here. |
| `coherence_exchange` | Connector/source-note mapped: intelligence-arbitrage and coherence/liquidity framing connecting orchestration to verification supply chains, reviewable knowledge units, accounting mechanisms, and governance-visible knowledge movement. | Speculative connector context only; economic and institutional claims are not implemented or externally corroborated here. |
| `cognitive_compilation` | Passage-reviewed: DAG compilation, dependency analysis, scheduling, critical path and slack exposure, localized recompilation, validator-backed artifacts, repair locality, capability-tier routing, cycle rejection, and backpressure/resource budgets. | No local compiler, DAG benchmark, route-selection trace, or empirical ablation validates these benefits. |
| `tokenmana` | Passage-reviewed: budgets include governed capacity, load variance, latency, bounded burst controls, token-normalized cognitive friction, study design, privacy constraints, and quality/human-friction limits. | Economic model and study design only; no simulation, formal reproduction, usage data, or empirical welfare result exists here. |
| `moecot` | Connector/source-note mapped: runtime-reference context for routed specialist lanes, fail-closed ledgers, readiness gates, replay, residual tracking, promotion blockers, and handoff. | Source-reported runtime and benchmark artifacts have not been imported, inspected, or reproduced; this is not scheduler evidence. |

## Mechanism

PlanForge specializes the plan graph into a schedulable DAG. The PlanForge sources supply recursive decomposition, primitive typing, dependency inference, critical-path scheduling, MVI tier annotation, fallback, and failure-driven replanning. Cognitive Compilation connects the DAG to IR and localized repair; TokenMana keeps budgets broader than tokens; Coherence Exchange contributes speculative intelligence-arbitrage vocabulary; MoECOT remains runtime-reference context for routed lanes, ledgers, readiness gates, and replay.

A PlanForge DAG is the cost-quality control surface: every node has to name its capability tier, context request, budget, quality predicate, verification burden, route, escalation path, and residual.

The reviewed passages justify the architecture vocabulary without proving the scheduler. PlanForge supplies the DAG, MVI, scheduling, fallback, and watchdog terms; the compiler-architecture variant sharpens the compiler/orchestration frame but stores source-reported estimates in a compact raw export; Cognitive Compilation adds semantic-DAG scheduling, validator, capability-routing, and localized-repair mechanics; TokenMana keeps the budget surface from collapsing into token counts by adding load, latency, burst, friction, and privacy constraints. None of those passages show this repository selecting tiers, running a scheduler, reproducing source-reported savings, or measuring quality retention.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Accepted plan graph"] --> B["Primitive nodes"]
  B --> C["Dependency DAG"]
  C --> D["Capability tier annotation"]
  D --> E["Budget + risk annotation"]
  E --> F["Quality predicate"]
  F --> G["Worker / route assignment"]
  G --> H{"Predicate passes?"}
  H -- "yes" --> I["Merge artifact"]
  H -- "no" --> J["Escalate / residual"]
  I --> K["Cost-quality ledger"]
  J --> K
```

- Decompose goals into DAGs.
- Annotate nodes with capability tier, budget, context request, risk, and verification requirement.
- Merge redundant branches and escalate only when predicates fail.

The DAG should expose parallelism, critical path, redundant branches, cached work, and local repair opportunities. A failed node should not force global regeneration unless its dependencies require it. A passed node should not escape verification simply because it was assigned to a cheaper tier.

Cost accounting is evidence discipline, not marketing. A DAG may estimate tokens, time, tool cost, review cost, or latency, but any savings claim remains unpromoted until a reproducible run exists.

## Interfaces

The interface is the PlanForge DAG.

- Routing consumes capability annotations.
- VCM consumes context requests.
- Evidence records cost/quality results.

Minimum fields:

- `dag_id`
- `parent_plan`
- `nodes`
- `edges`
- `capability_tiers`
- `context_requests`
- `quality_predicates`
- `verification_requirements`
- `budget_limits`
- `route_assignments`
- `escalation_policy`
- `residuals`
- `cost_quality_ledger`

Routing consumes capability annotations. VCM consumes context requests. Evidence records cost, quality, residuals, and verification. Cognitive Compilation can later lower DAG nodes into semantic atoms or target-specific jobs.

## Invariants

- Dependency order is respected.
- Cost savings do not remove required verification.
- Escalation paths are explicit.

The efficiency invariant is bounded by verification: cost savings do not count if they delete required checks. A low-tier route can be correct only if the node's quality predicate and escalation policy remain intact.

## Failure modes

- Wrong tier selection.
- Dependency cycles.
- Economic optimization overriding safety.

Wrong tier selection produces cheap wrong work. Dependency cycles make a graph appear planned while it cannot dispatch. Economic optimization overriding safety is the Goodhart version of planning: the scheduler optimizes cost while silently erasing quality or authority requirements. Each failure should emit a residual, not a successful schedule.

## Minimal implementation

A DAG schema with node-level capability and verification annotations.

The smallest useful implementation is a `planforge_dag` schema and fixture. It should validate node and edge presence, capability tiers, quality predicates, verification requirements, budget limits, route assignments, escalation policy, residuals, and a cost-quality ledger. The accompanying Lean module is intentionally narrower than a scheduler: it models finite indexed plan graphs, requires a dispatchable graph record to carry an acyclicity certificate plus ordered dependency edges, and requires failed quality predicates to route to escalation or residual under a valid node-outcome predicate.

That fixture establishes the fields that a later scheduler must fill. The Lean predicates do not prove that an external planner constructed the graph correctly, that the selected tier is adequate, or that a cheaper route preserves quality.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| PlanForge DAG fixture validation | Check that the DAG fixture matches the public schema and includes nodes, edges, capability tiers, quality predicates, verification requirements, budgets, routes, escalation, residuals, and cost-quality ledger entries. | implemented by protocol validation; run in this pass |
| Indexed DAG dispatch predicate | Check that a dispatchable finite plan-graph record carries an acyclicity certificate and that every recorded dependency edge precedes its dependent. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.PlanForge`; builds locally |
| Capability tier assignment test | Check that each node has a minimum adequate tier, route assignment, and verification burden. | planned; not run |
| Failed-quality fallback predicate | Check that a failed quality predicate routes to escalation or residual under the valid node-outcome predicate. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.PlanForge`; builds locally |
| Scheduler-level DAG acyclicity test | Check that a deployed scheduler rejects cycles before dispatch. | planned; not run |

The fixture-shape check and finite-record proof predicates are implemented. Scheduler claims require graph algorithms, route-selection traces, quality predicates, and cost-quality results that can be reproduced.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:planforge.dag.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.PlanForge` | A dispatchable plan graph is acyclic and all dependencies precede dependents. | implemented |
| `lean:planforge.dag.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.PlanForge` | A node whose quality predicate fails must escalate or emit a residual. | implemented |

These proof targets are implemented only over finite Lean plan-graph and node-outcome records. `AsiStackProofs.PlanForge` checks that a dispatchable record carries an acyclicity certificate and ordered member edges, and that a failed quality predicate cannot satisfy the valid outcome predicate unless it escalates or emits a residual. MVI adequacy, scheduler behavior, and cost-quality dominance still require deterministic validators, empirical traces, or later formal models.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `planforge` | PlanForge | planning_control | Planning substrate. Goal-to-execution compilation, hierarchical decomposition, DAG planning, scheduling, intelligence arbitrage. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `planforge_compiler_arch` | PlanForge: A Compiler Architecture for AI Task Orchestration | planning_control | Later/alternate PlanForge framing. Prefer highest-quality/latest content after comparison. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `cognitive_compilation` | Cognitive Compilation | planning_semantic_ir | Compiler framing for LLM-centered planning, semantic IR, target compilation, incremental repair. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `tokenmana` | TokenMana | resource_economics | Regenerative capacity mechanisms for load-stable token pricing. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The crosswalk identifies sources for DAG orchestration, intelligence arbitrage, and resource governance. Four mappings have passage review, but the core claim stays at `argument` because connector mappings remain unreviewed and numerical savings, tier adequacy, scheduler behavior, quality retention, and welfare claims remain unpromoted until this repo records reproducible scheduler runs, route traces, simulations, or accepted evidence transitions.

## Summary

PlanForge DAGs and Intelligence Arbitrage turns planning into scheduled, typed work. The system decomposes goals, annotates nodes, routes by minimum adequate capability, verifies quality predicates, and records cost-quality outcomes.

The chapter's efficiency claim stays conservative: intelligence arbitrage is a design principle until reproducible scheduler experiments show cost and quality results. The architecture still benefits now because every node must name its tier, verification burden, budget, escalation path, and residuals. The next chapter lowers that scheduled work into semantic IR so artifact generation, validation, and repair have stable addresses.
